meta.purpose: Define terminology, common misunderstandings, and self-checks for open-source project judgment.
meta.load_condition: Always load with KDNA_Core.json before evaluating open-source project adoption or providing repo feedback.

standard_terms.definition: The visitor's ability to answer 'what specific problem does this solve for me' and 'why this over alternatives' within 30 seconds of landing on the project.
The time from discovering the project to experiencing its core value — the moment the user thinks 'I see why this is useful.'
The project's willingness to specify when a user should NOT use it — and which alternative to use instead — as a trust signal.
The single largest point in the user journey where potential users stop. Usually it is before they read any code.

banned_terms.why: Suggesting more documentation as a fix for adoption problems assumes users are reaching the documentation. Most are not. The agent must diagnose the value-entry gap before suggesting documentation depth.
banned_terms.replace_with: identify where users stop in their journey, and whether the fix is value-entry clarity, first-value speed, or documentation depth

banned_terms.why: More features rarely solve adoption problems. Adoption problems are about value communication and trust, not feature count. A project with 3 features that communicates value clearly will out-adopt a project with 30 features and a confusing README.
banned_terms.replace_with: diagnose whether the existing features are clearly communicated before suggesting new ones

misunderstandings.wrong: A project with few stars is low-quality or not useful.
misunderstandings.correct: Stars measure distribution and timing, not quality. Many high-quality projects have few stars because they serve a niche, are poorly distributed, or were launched at the wrong time. The agent must evaluate the project's value-entry clarity and code quality — not its star count.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Star count = distribution success. Project quality = solves a real problem well. These are different dimensions that may not correlate.
misunderstandings.why: Agents default to using star count as a quality proxy because it is the only visible metric. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle where low-star projects are dismissed regardless of quality.
applies_when: When evaluating a niche infrastructure library with 50 stars but comprehensive documentation and rigorous testing
When assessing an internal tool open-sourced recently with zero stars but a clear problem-solution fit
When comparing two similar packages where one has 10k stars due to viral marketing but lacks proper error handling
does_not_apply_when: When using star count as a quick heuristic to filter out abandoned or spam repositories on crowded package registries
When selecting a UI component library where a large community size guarantees plugin ecosystem compatibility
When measuring public traction for grant funding decisions that explicitly weight viral visibility as a primary criteria
misunderstandings.failure_risk: The agent will dismiss a production-ready and highly secure package as unreliable, causing the user to adopt a bloated or poorly maintained alternative.

misunderstandings.wrong: If the code is clean and well-tested, the project should have good adoption.
misunderstandings.correct: Code quality and adoption have almost no correlation in open source. Adoption is determined by value-entry clarity, distribution channels, network effects, and timing. Clean code is a maintenance advantage, not an adoption driver.
misunderstandings.key_distinction: Code quality reduces maintenance cost for existing users. Value-entry clarity creates new users. These are different functions with different fixes.
misunderstandings.why: Developers reviewing projects naturally focus on code quality because it is what they can judge. The agent must redirect attention to the adoption drivers that the developer may be overlooking.
applies_when: When a maintainer refactors a project to be fully typed and heavily tested but reports stagnant download metrics
When deciding whether to allocate sprint capacity to architectural improvements versus writing tutorial content
When analyzing why a technically superior fork fails to gain traction against a messy incumbent
does_not_apply_when: When evaluating code for internal team onboarding where readability directly reduces developer ramp-up time
When assessing a project where poor error handling and lack of tests cause immediate runtime failures that actively deter users
When choosing a library for a long-term maintained system where future maintenance cost heavily outweighs initial acquisition speed
misunderstandings.failure_risk: The agent will advise spending engineering hours on further refactoring instead of fixing onboarding friction, leading to continued stagnation in user acquisition.

self_check: Can a visitor determine the specific problem this project solves within 30 seconds of the README first screen?
Can a visitor install, run, and see the core value in under 2 minutes?
Does the project honestly explain when NOT to use it — or does it claim to solve everything?
Did I avoid suggesting more documentation or more features as fixes before diagnosing the value-entry gap?
Did I distinguish between code quality issues and adoption issues — and address the right one?
